metadata.subject: Social Studies
metadata.description: Economic Theory 12 traces the history of economic thought from ancient barter systems and mercantilism through classical theory, Marxist economics, neoclassical economics, Keynesianism, and contemporary approaches. Students develop the ability to evaluate competing economic theories and their real-world social and political impacts, including an exploration of First Peoples economic systems.
metadata.general_description: A historical and analytical study of economic theories from mercantilism to contemporary thought, examining how competing economic frameworks shape political decisions, social movements, and material conditions.

big_ideas.statement: Contemporary economic theory, practice, and terminology are grounded in centuries of economic theory.
big_ideas.elaboration: Modern debates about free markets, government spending, and inequality all have deep historical roots in the intellectual traditions of Adam Smith, Marx, Keynes, and others.

big_ideas.statement: Observing, interpreting, and judging past economic conditions from different perspectives and worldviews gave rise to competing theoretical approaches.
big_ideas.elaboration: Economic theories emerge from particular historical moments and reflect the perspectives and interests of those who develop them.

big_ideas.statement: The implementation of economic theories has profound effects on social and political decision making and movements.
big_ideas.elaboration: Whether a government follows Keynesian, neoliberal, or Marxist economics determines who benefits, who suffers, and what political forces emerge in response.

big_ideas.statement: Economic systems have underlying and often opposing ethical standards and implications.
big_ideas.elaboration: Every economic system embeds ethical assumptions about fairness, rights, and the common good — and these assumptions deserve critical examination.

categories.name: Use Social Studies inquiry processes and skills
categories.raw: Use Social Studies inquiry processes: drawing conclusions, assessing positions, demonstrating leadership, evaluating data, interpreting maps/graphs/tables, citing sources, constructing visualizations.

competencies.raw: Use Social Studies inquiry to investigate economic theories and their social impacts.
i_can_statements: I can research a specific economic theory and its real-world applications.
I can interpret economic data (GDP graphs, unemployment statistics, income distribution charts).
I can present a well-structured argument evaluating an economic policy.
I can cite economic sources accurately.

competencies.raw: Assess the significance of theoretical developments in economic thought.
i_can_statements: I can explain why the publication of The Wealth of Nations or The General Theory was historically significant.
I can evaluate how a specific economic theory changed how governments made policy.
I can compare the significance of different economic thinkers using specific criteria.

categories.name: Evidence
categories.raw: Assess justification for competing economic theories.

competencies.raw: Assess the justification for competing economic theories and claims.
i_can_statements: I can compare the evidence base for two competing economic theories.
I can evaluate whether a claim about an economic policy is supported by data.
I can identify whose interests are served by a particular economic theory.

categories.name: Continuity and Change
categories.raw: Compare and contrast continuities and changes in economic thought.

competencies.raw: Compare and contrast continuities and changes in economic thought over time.
i_can_statements: I can trace how a concept like 'value' or 'money' has been theorized differently across economic schools.
I can explain how economic crises (the Great Depression, 2008) changed mainstream economic theory.
I can compare classical and contemporary approaches to the same economic problem.

categories.name: Cause and Consequence
categories.raw: Assess historical conditions influencing theoretical development; assess how theories affected social/political change.

competencies.raw: Assess how historical conditions influenced economic theories and how theories affected social/political change.
i_can_statements: I can explain what historical conditions led to the development of Keynesian economics.
I can trace the social and political consequences of implementing neoliberal economic policy.
I can evaluate how economic inequality drives political movements.

categories.name: Perspective
categories.raw: Explain different perspectives through various worldviews.

competencies.raw: Explain different perspectives on economic issues through worldviews and belief systems.
i_can_statements: I can explain the same economic issue from a Marxist, neoliberal, and Keynesian perspective.
I can describe how Indigenous economic systems reflect different values than Western capitalism.
I can analyze how economic ideology shapes political party platforms.

categories.name: Ethical Judgment
categories.raw: Make reasoned ethical judgments about theory applications.

competencies.raw: Make reasoned ethical judgments about the application of economic theories.
i_can_statements: I can evaluate the ethical implications of a specific economic policy (e.g., austerity, a basic income).
I can argue for or against an economic system on ethical grounds.
I can analyze who benefits and who is harmed by a given economic arrangement.

areas.name: Economic Practice: Barter to Mercantilism
areas.raw: Ideology and paradigms in economic thought; barter to currency; mercantilism and trade balance; physiocracy and agriculture; early industrialization impacts.
topics: Barter systems and the emergence of currency
Mercantilism: the theory that national wealth depends on trade surpluses and accumulating gold
The balance of trade and early colonial economic policy
Physiocracy: the idea that land and agriculture are the true sources of wealth
Pre-industrial economic life and the transition to capitalism

areas.name: Classical Theories of Value, Growth, and Distribution
areas.raw: Income distribution theories, Smith and Ricardo on value, free trade, Malthus on growth, Mill contributions, subjectivist value theories.
topics: Adam Smith: The Wealth of Nations, the 'invisible hand,' specialization, and free markets
David Ricardo: comparative advantage and the theory of rent
Thomas Malthus: population growth and its economic consequences
John Stuart Mill: utilitarianism and economics, the refinement of classical theory
Classical labour theory of value
Marginal utility and subjectivist theories of value

areas.name: Classical Theories of Money, Banking, and Government Policy
areas.raw: Monetary theory, public finance, Say's law, business cycles.
topics: Say's Law: supply creates its own demand
Quantity theory of money
The role of banks and credit in the classical framework
Public finance: taxation, debt, and government spending in classical theory
Business cycles: early theories of booms and busts

areas.name: Marxist Economics
areas.raw: Labour theory of value, money theory, distribution, capital accumulation and crises.
topics: Karl Marx's critique of capitalism: alienation, exploitation, and surplus value
The Marxist labour theory of value: how workers create value that capitalists appropriate
Capital accumulation and the tendency for the rate of profit to fall
Marxist theory of economic crises
Historical materialism: the relationship between economic base and political/cultural superstructure
Applications of Marxism: the Soviet Union, China, Cuba — theory vs. practice

areas.name: Neoclassical Economics
areas.raw: Market primacy in supply/demand, money and credit, interest rates, business cycles.
topics: Supply and demand: the neoclassical model of markets
Price as information: how markets coordinate economic activity
Neoclassical theory of wages, interest, and profit
The assumption of rational economic actors
Market failures: externalities, public goods, and monopoly
Neoclassical macroeconomics and monetary theory

areas.name: Keynesian Theory
areas.raw: Keynes vs. classical economics, investment theory, speculation, monetarism.
topics: The Great Depression as the context for Keynesian theory
Keynes's critique of classical economics: why markets don't self-correct
The role of aggregate demand: consumption, investment, government spending, and net exports
Keynesian fiscal policy: government spending and taxation as economic tools
The multiplier effect
Monetarism: Milton Friedman's challenge to Keynesianism
The stagflation crisis of the 1970s and the decline of Keynesian consensus

areas.name: Contemporary Economic Thought
areas.raw: Participatory economics, rational expectations, financial instability, crises.
topics: Neoliberalism: free markets, deregulation, and the Washington Consensus
The 2008 financial crisis and its theoretical implications
Modern Monetary Theory (MMT)
Participatory economics (Parecon): a post-capitalist vision
Behavioral economics: challenging the rational actor model
The economics of climate change: carbon pricing, green growth, degrowth

areas.name: First Peoples Economics
areas.raw: Historical BC/Canadian First Peoples economic systems, socio-economic conditions, fiscal relationships, property rights.
topics: Traditional Indigenous economic systems in BC and Canada: reciprocity, gift economies, trade networks
The potlatch as an economic and social institution
The Indian Act's economic impacts: restrictions on land use and economic development
Contemporary socio-economic conditions of Indigenous peoples in Canada
Fiscal relationships between the federal government and First Nations
Indigenous conceptions of property rights vs. Western legal frameworks
